preamble.summary: Exodus in the SP contains some of the most extensive expansions in the entire Pentateuch. The plague narrative receives systematic harmonistic additions where God's commands to Moses are supplemented with Moses' execution reports (and vice versa). The Decalogue in Exodus 20 is the site of the SP's distinctive 10th commandment mandating worship at Gerizim. Altar-command passages throughout the book receive Gerizim insertions.
preamble.notable_variants: The single most important variant is the SP 10th commandment added after Exodus 20:17, commanding the building of an altar on Mount Gerizim. The plague narratives (Ex 7-12) contain massive harmonistic expansions. Exodus 32 (golden calf) has minor variants. Every passage mentioning 'the place the LORD will choose' is adjusted to support Gerizim theology.
preamble.theological_significance: SP Exodus reveals the Samaritan claim that the Gerizim commandment is original to Sinai revelation, not a later addition. The harmonistic expansions in the plague narrative show SP's concern for narrative consistency — ensuring divine commands and their fulfillment are explicitly matched. The Passover legislation (Ex 12) contains variants that affect liturgical practice.

variants.mt_rendering: Gershom ... 'I have been a stranger in a foreign land'
variants.sp_rendering: Gershom ... 'I have been a stranger in a foreign land'
notes: Minor orthographic variant in the spelling of Gershom. Content is identical.

variants.sp_rendering: one hundred and thirty-seven years
notes: Amram's lifespan agrees between MT and SP. The genealogical figures in Exodus are more stable than those in Genesis.

variants.sp_reading: Expanded form: adds Moses and Aaron going to Pharaoh and delivering the message
variants.sp_rendering: (SP adds execution: Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and said...)
notes: SP's plague narrative systematically harmonizes divine command and human execution. Where MT has only God's instruction, SP adds the account of Moses carrying it out, and vice versa. This pattern recurs through all ten plagues and represents the largest block of expansive material in the SP.

variants.sp_reading: SP adds fulfillment report matching the command
variants.sp_rendering: (SP adds: And Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh...)
notes: Another harmonistic expansion in the plague of frogs. SP ensures the reader sees both the command and its fulfillment explicitly.

variants.sp_reading: Plague of gnats — expanded with warning to Pharaoh
variants.sp_rendering: (SP adds a warning speech to Pharaoh before the plague)
notes: In MT, the gnats plague comes without warning. SP adds a warning speech parallel to the other plagues, creating formal consistency across all ten plagues.

variants.mt_reading: Plague of flies — standard MT form
variants.sp_rendering: (Expanded with harmonistic additions)
notes: SP continues its pattern of harmonizing command and fulfillment throughout the flies plague.

variants.sp_reading: Plague on livestock — expanded with parallel warning
variants.sp_rendering: (Expanded with fulfillment report)
notes: The livestock plague receives the same harmonistic treatment. SP's expansions in Exodus 7-12 total several hundred words of additional text.

variants.sp_reading: Hail plague — expanded warning and fulfillment
variants.sp_rendering: (Expanded with added detail)
notes: SP continues the harmonistic expansion pattern in the hail plague.

variants.sp_reading: Locust plague — expanded with execution report
variants.sp_rendering: (Expanded with Moses delivering the message to Pharaoh)
notes: The locust plague receives harmonistic expansion consistent with SP's treatment of all plagues.

variants.mt_reading: Death of firstborn announcement — speech ends at 11:8
variants.sp_reading: Death of firstborn — expanded with fulfillment narrative
variants.mt_rendering: (Moses announces the plague and leaves Pharaoh)
variants.sp_rendering: (SP expands Moses' departure and adds transitional material)
notes: The final plague announcement receives the most elaborate SP expansion, befitting its climactic position.

variants.mt_reading: מוֹשַׁב בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר יָשְׁבוּ בְּמִצְרָיִם שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה
variants.sp_reading: מוֹשַׁב בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַאֲבוֹתָם אֲשֶׁר יָשְׁבוּ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן וּבְאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה
variants.mt_rendering: The dwelling of the children of Israel who dwelt in Egypt was four hundred and thirty years
variants.sp_rendering: The dwelling of the children of Israel and their fathers who dwelt in the land of Canaan and in the land of Egypt was four hundred and thirty years
notes: SP (and LXX) add 'and their fathers' and 'in the land of Canaan and,' making the 430 years cover both the patriarchal sojourn in Canaan AND the Egyptian slavery, not Egypt alone. This dramatically shortens the Egyptian bondage to approximately 215 years. Paul's argument in Galatians 3:17 depends on this longer reckoning. This is one of the most consequential chronological variants in the entire Bible.

variants.sp_rendering: leaven
notes: Minor orthographic variants in the unleavened bread legislation. Content is identical.

variants.mt_rendering: (Moses appoints leaders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens)
variants.sp_rendering: (SP adds minor harmonistic detail from Deut 1:15)
notes: SP harmonizes the Exodus account of judicial appointment with the Deuteronomy parallel, a minor example of its cross-referencing program.

variants.mt_rendering: I am the LORD your God
variants.sp_rendering: I am the LORD your God
notes: The opening of the Decalogue is identical in content. SP uses Samaritan script but the words are the same.

variants.mt_rendering: For in six days the LORD made
variants.sp_rendering: For in six days the LORD made
notes: The Sabbath commandment's creation rationale is identical in MT and SP. SP's distinctive Sabbath motivation from Deuteronomy 5 (exodus rather than creation) does not appear here.

variants.mt_rendering: You shall not murder / You shall not commit adultery
variants.sp_rendering: You shall not commit adultery / You shall not murder
notes: SP reverses the order of the murder and adultery commandments, placing adultery before murder. The LXX (in some manuscripts) and Nash Papyrus also show this order. This may reflect an older arrangement where sexual violations preceded violent ones.

variants.mt_reading: לֹא תַחְמֹד ... (coveting prohibition, end of Decalogue)
variants.sp_reading: לא תחמד ... (coveting prohibition, then 10th commandment follows)
variants.sp_rendering: You shall not covet... (9th commandment in SP numbering)
notes: In MT, the prohibition against coveting is the final commandment. In SP, it is the 9th, because SP adds a 10th commandment after it. See next entry.

variants.sp_reading: והיה כי יביאך יהוה אלהיך אל ארץ הכנעני ... והקמת לך אבנים גדלות ... בהר גריזים ... ובנית שם מזבח ליהוה אלהיך
variants.sp_rendering: And when the LORD your God brings you to the land of the Canaanites... you shall set up great stones... on Mount Gerizim... and you shall build there an altar to the LORD your God
notes: THE defining variant of the Samaritan Pentateuch. SP adds a 10th commandment to the Decalogue, compiled from Deuteronomy 11:29a, 27:2b-3a, 27:4a, 27:5-7, and 11:30. This commandment mandates the erection of inscribed stones and an altar on Mount Gerizim. In SP, this is part of the Sinai revelation itself, making Gerizim worship a foundational divine command rather than a later institution. The SP achieves its ten-commandment count by treating 'I am the LORD your God' as a commandment preamble (not a separate commandment) and combining the two coveting prohibitions into one.

variants.mt_rendering: in every place where I cause my name to be remembered
variants.sp_rendering: in the place where I have caused my name to be remembered
notes: MT reads 'every place' (כל המקום) with a future/ongoing sense; SP reads 'the place' (המקום) with a past tense, implying a single, already-designated location — Gerizim. This subtle variant transforms an open-ended altar law into a Gerizim-specific one.

variants.mt_rendering: the house of the LORD your God
variants.sp_rendering: the house of the LORD your God
notes: Text agrees, but Samaritan tradition reads 'house of the LORD' as the Gerizim sanctuary, not the Jerusalem temple.

variants.mt_rendering: And they saw the God of Israel
variants.sp_rendering: And they saw the glory of the God of Israel
notes: SP adds 'glory of' (כבוד) to avoid the anthropomorphism of seeing God directly. This is part of SP's anti-anthropomorphic tendency, which it shares with the LXX and Targumim.

variants.sp_reading: Minor numerical variants in tabernacle dimensions
variants.sp_rendering: (Slightly different measurements)
notes: SP has minor variants in several tabernacle measurements. These may reflect different architectural traditions or scribal errors in number transmission.

variants.sp_reading: Expanded furnishing list with additional detail
variants.sp_rendering: (Expanded furnishing details)
notes: SP occasionally adds minor details to the tabernacle instructions, harmonizing with the execution account in chapters 35-40.

variants.mt_rendering: And the people saw that Moses delayed
variants.sp_rendering: And the people saw that Moses delayed
notes: The golden calf narrative is substantially the same in MT and SP. SP does not soften Aaron's role or reduce Israel's culpability, contrary to what some scholars expected.

variants.sp_rendering: Now leave me alone
notes: God's anger speech in the golden calf incident is essentially identical in MT and SP.

variants.mt_rendering: And the LORD struck the people
variants.sp_rendering: And the LORD struck the people
notes: SP agrees with MT on divine punishment for the calf. No anti-anthropomorphic softening here.

variants.sp_rendering: (Minor harmonistic additions)
notes: SP adds small harmonistic touches to the covenant renewal, aligning it more closely with the Exodus 20-23 covenant.

variants.mt_reading: לֹא־תְבַעֲרוּ אֵשׁ בְּכֹל מֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם בְּיוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת
variants.sp_reading: לא תבערו אש בכל משבתיכם ביום השבת
variants.mt_rendering: You shall not kindle fire in any of your dwellings on the Sabbath day
variants.sp_rendering: You shall not kindle fire in any of your dwellings on the Sabbath day
notes: SP agrees with MT. Samaritan Sabbath observance interprets this strictly — no fire at all on Sabbath, unlike rabbinic Judaism which permits fire kindled before Sabbath.

variants.mt_reading: Construction account with some variations from instructions (Ex 25-31)
variants.sp_reading: Construction account systematically harmonized with instructions
variants.mt_rendering: (Construction narrative with minor discrepancies from blueprint)
variants.sp_rendering: (Construction narrative made to match blueprint exactly)
notes: SP systematically harmonizes the tabernacle construction account (Ex 36-39) with the divine instructions (Ex 25-31). Where MT has minor wording differences between command and fulfillment, SP eliminates them. This is the largest single block of harmonistic revision in SP Exodus, involving hundreds of small changes.

variants.sp_rendering: as the LORD commanded Moses
notes: This refrain appears throughout Ex 36-39. SP and MT agree on the refrain itself, though SP adjusts the surrounding text to make the correspondence between command and execution more precise.

variants.mt_rendering: in the first month in the second year
variants.sp_rendering: in the first month in the second year
notes: The date of the tabernacle's erection agrees in MT and SP.
